report_name: New 'GreatXML' Zero-Day Exploit Bypasses Windows BitLocker Encryption, Researcher Claims

threat_actors.name: Chaotic Eclipse
threat_actors.description: A security researcher who publicly disclosed the 'GreatXML' zero-day exploit on June 10, 2026, as part of a public dispute with Microsoft over its vulnerability handling and bug bounty program.

attack_patterns.name: Exploitation for Privilege Escalation
attack_patterns.description: The GreatXML exploit allows an attacker with physical access to gain a command prompt with SYSTEM privileges within the Windows Recovery Environment (WinRE).

attack_patterns.name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution
attack_patterns.description: The exploit abuses the Windows Recovery Environment (WinRE) boot sequence by creating a malicious `unattend.xml` file on the recovery partition, which is automatically parsed to execute a command shell.

attack_patterns.name: Disable or Modify Tools
attack_patterns.description: The GreatXML exploit bypasses the Windows BitLocker full-disk encryption security feature, undermining its ability to protect data at rest.

tools.name: GreatXML
tools.description: A zero-day exploit that bypasses Windows BitLocker by abusing XML artifacts in the Windows Recovery Environment (WinRE) to gain SYSTEM-level privileges.

vulnerabilities.name: GreatXML BitLocker Bypass
vulnerabilities.description: An unpatched zero-day local privilege escalation and security feature bypass vulnerability in the Windows Recovery Environment (WinRE). An attacker with physical access can place a malicious unattend.xml file on the recovery partition, which is parsed on boot to grant SYSTEM-level access, bypassing BitLocker encryption. The vulnerability requires a Microsoft Defender offline scan to have been previously run.

identities.name: Microsoft
identities.description: Vendor of the affected products (Windows, BitLocker, Microsoft Defender). The company is in a public dispute with the researcher who disclosed the vulnerability.

indicators.name: GreatXML Exploit Attempt via unattend.xml
indicators.pattern:
title: GreatXML Exploit Attempt via unattend.xml
id: 2b9e1f0a-3c8d-4b7e-9f6a-1d2c3b4a5e6f
status: experimental
description: Detects the creation of a potentially malicious unattend.xml file in a recovery partition path, which is a primary indicator of the GreatXML BitLocker bypass exploit.
references:
  - https://www.securityweek.com/greatxml-zero-day-exploit-bypasses-bitlocker/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-11
tags:
  - attack.t1547
level: high
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    TargetFilename|endswith: '\Recovery\unattend.xml'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects the creation of unattend.xml on a recovery partition, a key step in the GreatXML exploit.

indicators.name: Suspicious Reboot to Windows Recovery Environment
indicators.pattern:
title: Suspicious Reboot to Windows Recovery Environment
id: 8d7c6b5a-4e3f-4a9b-8c7d-6f5e4d3c2b1a
status: experimental
description: Detects the use of 'shutdown /r /o' command, which reboots the computer to the advanced boot options menu. This is a necessary step for the GreatXML exploit and can be anomalous in many user environments.
references:
  - https://www.securityweek.com/greatxml-zero-day-exploit-bypasses-bitlocker/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-11
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate administrative or user troubleshooting activity.
tags:
  - attack.t1068
level: medium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\shutdown.exe'
    CommandLine|contains|all:
      - '/r'
      - '/o'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects the command used to reboot into the Windows Recovery Environment, a prerequisite for the GreatXML attack.

relationships.source_name: Suspicious Reboot to Windows Recovery Environment
